data.content:
All data stores

are write-only

(except possibly Drugs)

and can be joined

Infra needs:

  1. compute cluster to fire rules (likely Kubernetes?)
  2. Azure Service Bus for events
  3. Azure Cloud Functions for UI Gateway
  4. Redis or similar cache for UI Gateway
  5. MS-SQL OLTP database
  6. Aggregated logging (& Monitoring, later)
  7. Queryable cold storage for providers, eligibility and old cases (tables stored as parquet?)
  8. Document Store for rules?
  9. OAuth Identity Federation via Okta?
  10. Databricks workspaces (prod and nonprod) and Unity catalog for Migration and Reporting pipelines

From Neal Ruggles:

When standing up the services, we have this package to abstract a lot of the minutia of setting up a service. It'd be awesome if you can re-use it to keep all new infrastructure consistent!

 

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-common-api

 

Consuming it just involves defining a ServiceDefinition in the root of the API proj:

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-api-notification-service?path…

 

and in Program.cs, you extend BaseService:

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-api-notification-service?path…

 

you get DI registration, Okta AuthN/AuthZ, KeyVault, Redis, FeatureFlags (via env configs), and Swagger for free

 

For DI, you just define a Bootstrapper file at each layer (API, Business, Client, Data, etc.) and the startup automatically registers everything:

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-api-notification-service?path…

New concept for case storage:

  1. Most case state in per-case SQLite files stored as blobs, owned entirely by Case CRUD component. Records are write-only.
  2. Case SQLite includes copies of write-only records from other schemas. If one of those updates outside the event, create an event to update the case and fire rules. Case engine will never modify these records; other schemas are source-of-truth.
  3. Most schema migrations can be handled lazily by writing C# functions to set values for new fields (never alter old fields)
  4. Case Meta Schema is not write-only and contains:
  5. path to SQLite blob
  6. case ID
  7. member ID
  8. payer ID
  9. provider IDs
  10. episode ID?
  11. drug IDs
  12. lock for SQLite blob? (or use Redis)
  13. Case Meta Schema also contains a write-only table relating case IDs to update metadata (config version, etc)
  14. The Case CRUD layer can merge SQLite files for cases in the same episode.


Advantages:

  1. case store is fully scalable in volume and accesses
  2. case data can't be split up by accident
  3. hot/cold storage can be handled at infrastructure layer
  4. core data model encapsulated

Drawbacks:

  1. to reverse a bad configuration, must open each SQLite file
  2. need custom migration logic
  3. need to maintain bulk ingest APIs
OLD DRAFT - Sample Case Engine Workflow Diagrams 1/12/26
Good use of AI to automatically parse/sort doc, but that's out of scope for now.

Should the Queue Manager touch the Case itself?
e.g. modify priority? Maybe. It might be best to treat the queuing system as a kind of control plane for a case. If priority changes, use Case CRUD.
Data is transferred to clients through a mix of push and pull mechanisms
source data quality gaps magnify work and risk
Enhancement: Data Activation Solution for creating managed data exports to clients
Enhancement: Operational Metrics for Data Product Manufacturing
Enhancement: Standardizing on Delta Live Tables and metadata driven transformations to lower costs of developing data pipelines and ongoing maintenance
Enhancement: End to end data lineage to support proactive knowledge production graph administration for faster problem resolution
Enhancement: End to end data observability for outlier alerting and monitoring to maintain SLAs
Enhancement: Comprehensive single upstream data source to lower implementation costs
Enhancement: Data contracts where DNA is seen as 1st class customer to improve product quality

Summary Document

Overview

This document provides a concise summary of the infrastructure and services related to the Letters v2 project, focusing on data management, APIs, and related technologies.

Data Management

The data management infrastructure includes various SQL Server databases such as EligibilityMS, ProviderMS, DrugsMS, and LoggingDB. These databases support different aspects of the project, including eligibility, provider information, and drug data management. Additionally, there are multiple Blob Storage solutions for storing inbound faxes and Matis documents, along with other storage needs.

APIs and Services

Several APIs and services are integral to the system's functionality:

  1. Eligibility API: Manages eligibility-related queries and data processing.
  2. Provider API: Handles provider-related information and interactions.
  3. Drugs API: Facilitates drug data management and retrieval.
  4. Admin Portal API: Provides administrative functionalities and access control.
  5. Mail Service (Kaleidoscope): Manages email communications.
  6. IVR (Voice Recognition) API: Supports voice recognition capabilities.
  7. Turnaround Time (TaT) API: Monitors and reports on processing times.

Data Processing and ETL

The system employs various ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes to manage data flow and transformation:

  1. Azure Data Factory ETL and Databricks ETL: Facilitate data integration and processing.
  2. Salesforce ETL: Integrates Salesforce data into the system.
  3. Synapse ETL and SSIS ETL: Support data warehousing and integration tasks.

Additional Services

  1. EventProcessor Windows Service: Manages event-driven processes.
  2. MessageBridge Windows Service: Facilitates message routing and processing.
  3. Fax Service API: Handles fax communications.
  4. Document API: Manages document-related operations.

Security and Authentication

The system uses Okta for authentication and identity management, ensuring secure access to various services and APIs.

Communication and Integration

  1. Twilio: Provides communication capabilities, including SMS and voice services.
  2. Nuance: Supports advanced voice recognition and processing.

Monitoring and Reporting

  1. PowerBI: Used for data visualization and reporting.
  2. JobMonitor API: Tracks and reports on job statuses and performance metrics.
    This summary encapsulates the key components and services of the Letters v2 infrastructure, highlighting the integration of data management, APIs, and various supporting technologies.

data.title: Config & Rules whiteboard (Pero's notes and drafts)

data.content: Does some kind of evaluation happen here? If not, could it?
Validate inputs based on Ingest schema configuration?

Are eligibility checks a part of rules subsystem or a separate system?

- Yes, we will have access to their Elig microservice

Do any rules need to be defined on payer parent org? If so, do they take precedence over payer org level rules?
-Yes

- Lower level rule always take precedence
Assumption: Payer has only 1 parent payer org

Can patient belong to multiple employer groups at the same time?

- NO, but confirm with BSU
Case can have multiple provides. This logic should be triggered only for ones that are not eligible (OON) and find suitable replacement, right? What parameter to use?

Configuration

  1. Case workflow automation (business rules)
  2. Post-Decesion workflows
    1. Appeals (OOS)
    2. Re-opened cases
  3. Letter Formats
  4. Delegation
  5. Turnaround time calculations
    1. Assumptions: probably not in our SoW, but we need an understanding how it works and interacts
    2. working/non-working days
    3. status (when to pause and/or reset timer)
  6. Reports (aggregations of cases)
    1. OOS
  7. SSO
  8. Ingest Schema
    1. OOS
    2. SSIS packages etc (to get data from provider provided data dumps and migrate them to OH databases)
  9. Auth Interface - file schema or FHIR
    1. Sending auth files to payers in scope
      1. Not sure is FHIR in scope atm
    2. FHIR API interface can be used to share data with providers and payers
    3. While standardized, there are a lot of customizations, and these would need to be configured as data dictionary on our end
    4. TODO: ask Brendan about more details

Case status details:

  1. case can be saved & submitted, which can affect triggering of the rule

Next Steps:

  1. What are the specific data points that are needed in each section

Different payers, LoBs and Employer groups could have different requirements as to what patient, diagnostic and treatment details need to be entered (or collected). These requirements would be stored in configuration entity
Workflow configuration - basically we could have a custom workflow config for each client, with set of alowed state migrations and business rules that would be enforced at each step
Alternative config - one final step (determination) with multiple possible outcomes

Determination holds configurable number of outcomes, in this example:
- approved
- rejected
-partially approved
-whithdrawn
Currently there is no automated assignment and it's a big pain point

I can imagine designing a UI that works for internal users exclusively. 
Then basically "copying" it and stripping/modifying it to work for payers exclusively. 

From other perspectives: Why would we not want to do that?

UX POV (cb):
Can we establish an "eligibility badge" that stays with the patient case one eligibility is confirmed? Visible as a reminder that this check has been conducted and eliminates need to run check on each search?

UX POV (cb):
Can we establish the requirements for creating a member "account" v. "new case"? (We touched on this yesterday.) When does this happen? Does knowledge of that matter to end users?

ie XRT, GMT, chemo, surgical etc

These are bridge tables that add dosing.

They version with Regimen, so each new version of a regimen needs to write new element records. This is repetitious, but

avoids the confusion being able to change the elements of a regimen without incrementing the regimen version.

Eventually we will need to allow per-group Scope configuration (e.g. PA Exempt, Out-of-Scope). Admin Portal

allows config at Payer grain, but should also allow config at Employer Group grain.


Need more clarity about how config versions should relate to RxNorm versions.

Eventually may need a Diagnosis table,

with a DiagnosisRegimen bridge table to express which regimens are good with which diagnoses.

From Nick Foreyt:


We get an actual PersonIdentifier from most of them.  It is a "cradle to grave" / belly button identifier that persists with a person regardless of how many coverages they have.  I worked for a provider owned health plan up here in WI for 11 years and spent a TON of time on these items. 

 

I think what we do NOT want to get ourselves into, is matching the coverages and trying to derive a Person ID ourselves.  Very dangerous w/ patient safety, etc.

 

In my experience (from payer perspective):

  1.  Patient / MRN = Belly Button ID / Cradle to Grave
  2. Coverage / Plan = the Employer group purchasing insurance coverage from the payer
  3. Member = The combination of the Patient + Coverage.  A Patient becomes a member when they have effectuated insurance coverage to pay their claims.

Claims were actually attached to / billed to a Patient / MRN, not a coverage.  Then, our claims processing system looked up the coverage(s) available at the Point in Time of the claim.  We actually had derived coverage / member identifiers in our EDW because of retro coverage activity.

The MemberId can change for an eligibility record!

Payers use MemberIds in a wide variety of different ways, from being basically PersonIds to creating a new MemberId for every change in coverage. We do not want to build that variety into

our data model.

data.content:
NewUM Version 1.0 Product Scope [DRAFT] 

Summary 

NewUM Version 1.0 establishes the foundational platform for OncoHealth to deliver fully delegated chemotherapy utilization management services in Mainland across Commercial and Medicare lines of business. It achieves functional parity with existing OneUM platforms while introducing meaningful improvements in user experience, implementation efficiency, and clinical workflow and decision making. V1.0 enables OH teams to reliably intake, review, determine, and communicate decisions for chemotherapy treatment requests to health plans, providers, and members—within compliance timeframes—using a standardized, rules driven platform. 

This is not the final version intended for a live health plan launch. Instead, V1.0 delivers a broad enough scope to stand up a health plan in a production-like environment and conduct extensive user testing with complex cases—a critical step to derisk the first real health plan migration to NewUM. The target completion, per the SOW and project plans, is June 2026. 

This document is a product scoping guide for product, operational, and technical teams to make Version 1.0 decisions. It is intentionally concise and does not capture all product details; those will be managed with stakeholder input in Product Requirement Documents (PRDs) and NewUM roadmap artifacts. 

End-State Capabilities for Version 1.0 

Case Submission & Data Foundations 

  1. Providers and health plans can submit chemotherapy drug prior authorization cases through current intake methods (fax, phone, and web).  
  2. Treatment details (schedules, dosing, frequency, etc.) are captured using an industry-standard data structure with guardrails to improve accuracy and completeness. 
  3. The Admin Portal serves as the truth for all clinical data (drugs, regimens, diagnoses, etc.) and health plan specific drug coverage policies (prior authorization status, non-preferred drugs, etc.) and formulary file information for pharmacy drugs (quantity limits, step therapy). 
  4. Member eligibility and provider network data are sourced from the health plan's existing inbound data files.  

Clinical Review & Determinations 

  1. OH Operational teams (CRS, RNs, PharmDs, and MDs) can see complete protocol details within a case for medical necessity review, document clinical notes, record any required clinical interventions, and make final drug level determinations. The health plan's specific drug coverage policies are visible, actionable, and consistently applied within their review workflows.  
  2. The system automatically assigns drug level determinations for: 
  3. PA Exempt 
  4. Out of Scope (OOS) 
  5. Indication-based approvals 
  6. When all drugs in the case receive an automatic determination, NewUM auto-generates the complete notification package and auto-completes the case. 
  7. Clinical users can support health plans for appeals processing, but OH is not delegated to make these determinations within the platform. 
  8. Health plan and provider office staff users can search and view basic case details for pending and completed cases. 

Work Distribution 

  1. The system assigns cases to operational work queues based on case attributes and configurable routing rules, ensuring appropriate workload distribution across clinical teams. 
  2. Work distribution is configurable by an operational team (supervisor, manager) end user with minimal technical expertise. 

Turnaround Times (TAT) 

  1. All required Commercial and Medicare turnaround times are support through OH's TAT engine.  
  2. Users can see TAT status within the case and can support or prioritize cases in work queues based on upcoming deadlines.  

Notifications & Auth Files 

  1. Standard notification packages (letters and IVR) are generated and delivered based on configured business rules.  
  2. Following the completion of the case, the system triggers the appropriate, existing outbound authorization files to the health plan or PBM based on the drug's benefit type. 

Health Plan Configuration 

  1. Administrators can configure OH's standard offering for each health plan within the Admin Portal, including: 
  2. Drug policy configurations 
  3. Formularies 
  4. Delegation and benefit rules 
  5. Network and plan attributes 
  6. These configurations drive the guardrails and workflow logic within NewUM. 
  7. A configurable rules engine manageable by semitechnical users, with full versioning and change audit history so OH can see what rules exist, when they changed, and what was modified.  

Operational Insight 

  1. Basic operational KPIs are available via dashboards to support day-to-day oversight and performance monitoring.  
  2. A detailed case-level audit log capturing what changed, when, and by whom, including visibility into which rules and rule versions were applied during case processing. 

Medicare Specific Workflows 

  1. The following Medicare specific workflows are supported: 
  2. Appointment of Representative (AOR) 
  3. Untimely Determinations: 
  4. Deemed Denials (Part B) 
  5. Generate Documents for Independent Review Entity (IRE) (Part D) 
  6. Integrated Denial Notices (IDN) 
  7. Dismissal 
  8. Reconsiderations 
  9. Reopens 
  10. Dual Eligible Special Needs Plan (D-SNP) 
  11. Multi-Language Insert (MLI) 
  12. Notification Language and Accessibility Support 

Out of Scope 

  1. XRT and GMT 
  2. Delegated Appeals 
  3. Medicaid Workflows 
  4. Add-On UM Programs (e.g. Site of Care, Vial Rounding) 
  5. Puerto Rico Specific Workflows 
  6. Provider and Health Plan Portal Enhancements 
  7. Interop APIs for FHIR/CMS-0057 Compliance 
  8. Native AI-based Clinical Extraction (Anterior) 
  9. Advanced Provider Mastering (3rd Party PDM Service Interfaces)
data.html:
NewUM Version 1.0 Product Scope [DRAFT] 

Summary 

NewUM Version 1.0 establishes the foundational platform for OncoHealth to deliver fully delegated chemotherapy utilization management services in Mainland across Commercial and Medicare lines of business. It achieves functional parity with existing OneUM platforms while introducing meaningful improvements in user experience, implementation efficiency, and clinical workflow and decision making. V1.0 enables OH teams to reliably intake, review, determine, and communicate decisions for chemotherapy treatment requests to health plans, providers, and members—within compliance timeframes—using a standardized, rules driven platform. 

This is not the final version intended for a live health plan launch. Instead, V1.0 delivers a broad enough scope to stand up a health plan in a production-like environment and conduct extensive user testing with complex cases—a critical step to derisk the first real health plan migration to NewUM. The target completion, per the SOW and project plans, is June 2026. 

This document is a product scoping guide for product, operational, and technical teams to make Version 1.0 decisions. It is intentionally concise and does not capture all product details; those will be managed with stakeholder input in Product Requirement Documents (PRDs) and NewUM roadmap artifacts. 

End-State Capabilities for Version 1.0 

Case Submission & Data Foundations 

  1. Providers and health plans can submit chemotherapy drug prior authorization cases through current intake methods (fax, phone, and web).  
  2. Treatment details (schedules, dosing, frequency, etc.) are captured using an industry-standard data structure with guardrails to improve accuracy and completeness. 
  3. The Admin Portal serves as the truth for all clinical data (drugs, regimens, diagnoses, etc.) and health plan specific drug coverage policies (prior authorization status, non-preferred drugs, etc.) and formulary file information for pharmacy drugs (quantity limits, step therapy). 
  4. Member eligibility and provider network data are sourced from the health plan's existing inbound data files.  

Clinical Review & Determinations 

  1. OH Operational teams (CRS, RNs, PharmDs, and MDs) can see complete protocol details within a case for medical necessity review, document clinical notes, record any required clinical interventions, and make final drug level determinations. The health plan's specific drug coverage policies are visible, actionable, and consistently applied within their review workflows.  
  2. The system automatically assigns drug level determinations for: 
  3. PA Exempt 
  4. Out of Scope (OOS) 
  5. Indication-based approvals 
  6. When all drugs in the case receive an automatic determination, NewUM auto-generates the complete notification package and auto-completes the case. 
  7. Clinical users can support health plans for appeals processing, but OH is not delegated to make these determinations within the platform. 
  8. Health plan and provider office staff users can search and view basic case details for pending and completed cases. 

Work Distribution 

  1. The system assigns cases to operational work queues based on case attributes and configurable routing rules, ensuring appropriate workload distribution across clinical teams. 
  2. Work distribution is configurable by an operational team (supervisor, manager) end user with minimal technical expertise. 

Turnaround Times (TAT) 

  1. All required Commercial and Medicare turnaround times are support through OH's TAT engine.  
  2. Users can see TAT status within the case and can support or prioritize cases in work queues based on upcoming deadlines.  

Notifications & Auth Files 

  1. Standard notification packages (letters and IVR) are generated and delivered based on configured business rules.  
  2. Following the completion of the case, the system triggers the appropriate, existing outbound authorization files to the health plan or PBM based on the drug's benefit type. 

Health Plan Configuration 

  1. Administrators can configure OH's standard offering for each health plan within the Admin Portal, including: 
  2. Drug policy configurations 
  3. Formularies 
  4. Delegation and benefit rules 
  5. Network and plan attributes 
  6. These configurations drive the guardrails and workflow logic within NewUM. 
  7. A configurable rules engine manageable by semitechnical users, with full versioning and change audit history so OH can see what rules exist, when they changed, and what was modified.  

Operational Insight 

  1. Basic operational KPIs are available via dashboards to support day-to-day oversight and performance monitoring.  
  2. A detailed case-level audit log capturing what changed, when, and by whom, including visibility into which rules and rule versions were applied during case processing. 

Medicare Specific Workflows 

  1. The following Medicare specific workflows are supported: 
  2. Appointment of Representative (AOR) 
  3. Untimely Determinations: 
  4. Deemed Denials (Part B) 
  5. Generate Documents for Independent Review Entity (IRE) (Part D) 
  6. Integrated Denial Notices (IDN) 
  7. Dismissal 
  8. Reconsiderations 
  9. Reopens 
  10. Dual Eligible Special Needs Plan (D-SNP) 
  11. Multi-Language Insert (MLI) 
  12. Notification Language and Accessibility Support 

Out of Scope 

  1. XRT and GMT 
  2. Delegated Appeals 
  3. Medicaid Workflows 
  4. Add-On UM Programs (e.g. Site of Care, Vial Rounding) 
  5. Puerto Rico Specific Workflows 
  6. Provider and Health Plan Portal Enhancements 
  7. Interop APIs for FHIR/CMS-0057 Compliance 
  8. Native AI-based Clinical Extraction (Anterior) 
  9. Advanced Provider Mastering (3rd Party PDM Service Interfaces)

- Build eligibility ingestion pipeline - OOS for NF (ETLed by OH team)

- Add Member Search API (search + eligibility payload).

- Extend Case API for Draft state + eligibility validation rule.

- Rule Engine integration

Risks

  1. Member Search, Selection, & Coverage Determination, including Draft Case)
    1. Data
      1. Eligibility investigation and schema design may not be finished in time to unblock development.
      2. Dev environments currently lack usable anonymized data; some key tables are empty.
      3. No finalized process yet for anonymization, PHI usage, and transformation validation.
      4. Scope and feasibility of full data migration is unclear; partial migration will need a refill process for missing data.
      5. Performance tuning may be inaccurate if built on low‑volume or incomplete anonymized data.
      6. Strong dependency on OH Data team in case of knowledge transfer, especially once investigating legacy data model.
    2. Backend
      1. Rules engine integration is not finalized
    3. Frontend
      1. Missing final UX/UI designs increases risk of rework for member, diagnosis, and regimen screens.
      2. API not being ready or additional rework is needed
  2. Diagnosis Selection
    1. Backend:
      1. Diagnosis domain not yet modeled in new schema.
  3. Main Regimen Selection:
    1. Data
      1. DrugsMS schema complexity (84 tables)
  4. Allow Custom Regimens
    1. Backend
      1. Role-based enforcement enforcement is not finalized
  5. Apply Payer Drug Configurations
    1. Data
      1. Near-real-time update expectations for Payer Drug Library (PDL) changes
  6. Apply Non-Preferred Drug Rules
    1. Data:
      1. Reporting and switch-tracking requirements are not fully defined

data.title: Ensure that only diagnoses that are appropriate for Payer can be associated with that treatment type

data.content:
⚠️ Gaps & Missing Fields

1. Missing eligibility fields that scope determination requires. The docs explicitly require these fields on the Eligibility record, but the schema doesn't show them:

  1. CarrierId (string) — required for identifier-based scope validation
  2. AccountId / InsuranceGroupId / BenefitPackage — carve-out identifiers
  3. ASOIndicator (boolean) — ASO delegation rule
  4. MemberBenefit (enum: Medical / Pharmacy / Medical+Pharmacy)
  5. PrescriptionBenefitCarveout (boolean)
  6. OONBenefit (code)
  7. RiskPatient (flag)
  8. EligibilitySource (code)
  9. StateOfPlanIssue (state code)
  10. MedicareBeneficiaryId / MBI (string, Medicare-only)
  11. InsurancePlan / PlanDescription (string)
  12. ProductOffering (string/code — finer-grained than Product)
  13. PreferredLanguage, AccessibilityCorrespondence

The CustomFields (json) column on Eligibility could absorb some of these, but that's a red flag — scope determination rules need to query structured fields, not JSON blobs. Fields like MemberBenefit and ASOIndicator that drive hard business rules must be first-class columns.

2. Member contact data is missing. The docs require Address, Phone (home/mobile/work), and Member Representative contact info on the member/eligibility record. None of these appear in Person or Eligibility in the schema.

3. No CoverageEffective/CoverageEnd date pair visible on Eligibility — though these are shown in the schema, which is good. ✅ (Actually they are there.)

4. BusinessGrain's FK is PayerVersion (typed Type) — this looks like a data modeling bug. Type is not a valid FK type; it likely should be guid.

5. No explicit ProductOffering level. The docs describe a 3-level hierarchy: LOB → Product Line → Product Offering. The schema has MajorLineOfBusiness → Product, but the Product Offering granularity (needed for rule collision prevention per the docs) isn't clearly represented.

6. No MemberRepresentative entity. The docs specify representative name, relationship, address, and phone as required fields from eligibility. This would need its own table or fields on Person/Eligibility.

1. No patient height and weight fields. These are required inputs for every medical dose calculation. Without typed columns, the calculations cannot run or be validated.

2. No Treatment Start Date or Treatment End Date on the Case table. Treatment Start Date drives eligibility verification. Treatment End Date drives renewal eligibility. Neither has an explicit field — only a vague Time Due column exists.

3. Diagnosis Group is not stored. The requirements say it must be persisted on the case after the International Classification of Diseases version 10 code is selected. Without it, regimen search cannot be filtered and case entry cannot advance.

4. No renewal linkage field. The Case table has no reference to a prior case. This is required for auto-approval logic, deviation display, and audit reporting.

5. No drug change tracking fields. When a user switches from a non-preferred or branded drug to another, the original drug, the replacement, and the override reason must be recorded for financial impact reporting. No such fields exist.

6. Key calculated values are buried in a JSON blob. Allowed dose, maximum billing units, quantity per authorization, cycle count, and benefit type selection for crossover drugs are all likely stored inside the untyped Dosing field. They cannot be queried, validated, or enforced at the database level. These should be explicit columns.
Move from full data reload to upsert relevant tables approach
Still keep ablity to full data reload in case of any issues
